title: The Boy And The Angel
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Morning, evening, noon and night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Praise God!; sang Theocrite.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then to his poor trade he turned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereby the daily meal was earned.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hard he laboured, long and well;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er his work the boyן¿½s curls fell:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ever, at each period,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He stopped and sang, ן¿½Praise God!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then back again his curls he threw,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And cheerful turned to work anew.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said Blaise, the listening monk, ן¿½Well done;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I doubt not thou art heard, my son:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As well as if thy voice to-day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were praising God, the Popeן¿½s great way.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This Easter Day, the Pope at Rome
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Praises God from Peterן¿½s dome.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said Theocrite, ן¿½Would God that I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Might praise him, that great way, and die!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Night passed, day shone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Theocrite was gone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With God a day endures alway,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A thousand years are but a day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God said in heaven, ן¿½Nor day nor night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now brings the voice of my delight.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Gabriel, like a rainbowן¿½s birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spread his wings and sank to earth;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Entered, in flesh, the empty cell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lived there, and played the craftsman well;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And morning, evening, noon and night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Praised God in place of Theocrite.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from a boy, to youth he grew:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The man put off the striplingן¿½s hue:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The man matured and fell away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the season of decay:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ever oן¿½er the trade he bent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ever lived on earth content.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (He did Godן¿½s will; to him, all one
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If on the earth or in the sun.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God said, ן¿½A praise is in mine ear;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There is no doubt in it, no fear:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So sing old worlds, and so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½New worlds that from my footstool go.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Clearer loves sound other ways:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I miss my little human praise.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then forth sprang Gabrielן¿½s wings, off fell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The flesh disguise, remained the cell.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas Easter Day: he flew to Rome,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And paused above Saint Peterן¿½s dome.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the tiring-room close by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The great outer gallery,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his holy vestments dight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stood the new Pope, Theocrite:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all his past career
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came back upon him clear,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since when, a boy, he plied his trade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till on his life the sickness weighed;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in his cell, when death drew near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An angel in a dream brought cheer:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And rising from the sickness drear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He grew a priest, and now stood here.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the East with praise he turned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on his sight the angel burned.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I bore thee from thy craftsmanן¿½s cell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And set thee here; I did not well.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vainly I left my angel-sphere,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vain was thy dream of many a year.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thy voiceן¿½s praise seemed weak; it dropped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Creationן¿½s chorus stopped!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go back and praise again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The early way, while I remain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With that weak voice of our disdain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Take up Creationן¿½s pausing strain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Back to the cell and poor employ:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Resume the craftsman and the boy!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theocrite grew old at home;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A new Pope dwelt in Peterן¿½s dome.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One vanished as the other died:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They sought God side by side.
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
